title: Research
text: My research is centered on explainable AI, mechanistic interpretability, uncertainty quantification, and language-model reliability.

title: Research
text:
Research agenda
I am interested in explanations as operational signals: not only as post-hoc descriptions for humans, but as measurable quantities that can improve AI systems.
Current directions:

detecting contextual hallucinations in retrieval-augmented generation,
using model-internal features for evaluation and reliability,
explanation-based covariate drift detection,
interpretable NLP systems for information extraction and document understanding,
reliable AI workflows for science and industry.

title: Research
text:
Explainability as an engineering signal
A recurring theme in my work is that explanations can be used to improve AI systems, not only to explain them after the fact. This includes model monitoring, drift detection, model evaluation, and mechanistic diagnostics.

title: Research
text:
Software Campus
Software Campus Fellow / Project Lead
Industry partner: Celonis
I am developing explainability-driven methods for evaluating and improving AI systems, with an emphasis on language-model reliability, grounding, and mechanistic diagnostics.

title: Research
text:
Collaboration interests
I am especially interested in source-grounded AI-for-science settings where model outputs should be constrained by provided evidence:

biomedical and clinical QA,
mathematical or scientific reasoning,
engineering and technical documentation,
policy, legal, and financial reports,
enterprise process and operational documents.

title: Projects
text:
Grounding via Head-wise Output Gram Features
GroundHOG is a method for detecting contextual hallucination in retrieval-augmented question answering. It decomposes model-internal writes into three sources:

retrieved context,
response history,
feed-forward / parametric computation.

The method compares the magnitudes and interactions of these writes using per-head Gram features, then uses sparse classifiers or training-free readouts to detect when a generated answer is unsupported by the source.


For scientific and high-stakes RAG systems, the practical question is often not only whether an answer sounds true, but whether each claim is grounded in the documents provided to the model.



This project was adapted into a collaboration-oriented poster for the AI Grid Summer School “AI for Science”, Paris 2026.

title: Projects
text:
Explanation-based drift detection
This work studies how explanation signals can be used to identify covariate drift. The motivation is to use interpretability methods as practical model-monitoring tools rather than only as human-facing explanations.

title: Projects
section: Entity linking for product carbon footprint estimation
text:
Entity linking for product carbon footprint estimation
This project applies language models and entity linking to map manufactured components to environmental impact databases, supporting automated product carbon footprint estimation.

title: Steffen Castle
text:
Explainable AI · Mechanistic Interpretability · Reliable Language Models
I study how language models use information internally, and how those internal signals can be used to detect failures such as contextual hallucinations in retrieval-augmented generation.

title: Steffen Castle
text:
Research story


2019–2020
Improving neural network explanations
Master’s research on Pattern-Guided Attribution and Pattern-Guided Integrated Gradients.


2021
Using explanations for drift detection
Explanation signals as operational tools for detecting covariate shift.


2024–present
PhD: explanations as signals to improve AI systems
Using model explanations for evaluation, monitoring, and reliability.


Current
Mechanistic grounding and hallucination detection
Detecting unsupported RAG answers from source-specific residual-stream writes.
